sections.heading: Life, Controversy, and Martyrdom
sections.html:
Early Life and Entry to Monasticism

Maximus was born around 580, likely in Constantinople, into a noble family of considerable influence. He received an excellent classical education and rose to the position of first secretary (prôtasêkrêtis) to the Emperor Heraclius. Yet despite this proximity to imperial power, Maximus felt drawn to the contemplative life. Around 614 he withdrew from court and entered the monastery of Chrysopolis (Scutari) near Constantinople. Later, fleeing the Persian and then Arab invasions, he moved to North Africa, where he encountered the vibrant monastic and theological community of Carthage. It was here that his mature theological work was forged — in conversation with monks, bishops, and refugees — and where he became increasingly aware of the doctrinal crisis gripping the Eastern Church.

Maximus's intellectual formation drew on the full range of the Greek theological tradition: the Cappadocian Fathers, Cyril of Alexandria, the Pseudo-Dionysius, and above all Origen's theological instincts purified of their errors. His monastic ascesis was the soil from which his speculative theology grew — he never treated doctrine as merely academic but always as the grammar of the soul's ascent to God.

The Monothelite Controversy

The central controversy of Maximus's life concerned whether Christ possessed one will or two. Monothelitism — the doctrine of one will — had been promoted as a political compromise to reunite the Chalcedonian and Monophysite factions in the Eastern Empire. The Ecthesis (638) of Emperor Heraclius and later the Typos (648) of Constans II imposed Monothelitism on the Church by imperial decree.

Maximus recognized immediately that Monothelitism was not merely a political compromise but a theological catastrophe. If Christ has only one will, then either his human will is absorbed into the divine (a form of Monophysitism in the realm of the will), or the question of how Christ truly represents humanity in his obedience to the Father becomes incoherent. For Maximus, Christ's two wills — divine and human — are essential to our salvation. The human will of Christ, by freely obeying the Father in Gethsemane — "not my will, but yours, be done" (Luke 22:42) — recapitulates and heals Adam's willful disobedience. Without a genuine human will in Christ, human freedom is not redeemed but bypassed.

This is not a merely technical point. It touches the heart of Christology and soteriology. If the Son of God did not assume a human will, then the human will — the seat of sin and of salvation — was not taken up into the healing union of the Incarnation. The ancient soteriological axiom, formulated by Gregory Nazianzus and cited repeatedly by Maximus, applies with full force: "What is not assumed is not healed."

  • Heb 4:15 — Christ tempted in every way as we are, yet without sin — implying a genuine human will capable of temptation.
  • Phil 2:8 — Christ "became obedient to the point of death" — obedience requires a will.
  • Matt 26:39 — The Gethsemane prayer as the supreme expression of Christ's human will subordinating itself to the Father.

Trial, Mutilation, Exile, and Vindication

In 649 Maximus played a decisive role at the Lateran Council in Rome convened by Pope Martin I, which condemned Monothelitism and the imperial Typos. Both Martin and Maximus were subsequently arrested by imperial order. Martin died in exile in Crimea in 655. Maximus was tried before an imperial court, refused to accept Monothelitism despite enormous pressure, and was condemned. In 662, at over eighty years of age, he suffered the horrific penalty prescribed for those who persisted in "heresy" against the imperial will: his tongue and his right hand were cut off — the instruments of his teaching and writing — and he was exiled to Lazica on the eastern shore of the Black Sea, where he died the same year.

The vindication Maximus sought in life came nineteen years after his death. The Third Council of Constantinople (680–681), the Sixth Ecumenical Council, formally defined Dyothelitism — two wills in Christ — as the orthodox faith, and anathematized the Monothelite patriarchs and the emperor who had promoted the heresy. Maximus was posthumously recognized as the great champion of Chalcedonian Christology and was venerated as a Confessor — one who bore witness to the faith through suffering short of martyrdom, though his death from his wounds in exile placed him on the boundary of martyrdom as well.

sections.heading: Deification and the Cosmic Christ
sections.html:
Theosis as the Goal of Creation

At the center of Maximus's theology stands the doctrine of theosis — deification. The purpose of creation, and supremely of the Incarnation, is that human beings (and through them the entire cosmos) should come to participate in the divine nature (2 Pet 1:4). This is not a pantheistic fusion with God but a participation in God's energies and life that leaves the creature's nature intact while elevating it beyond all natural capacity. Maximus expresses this with characteristic precision: we become God by grace, not by nature; we share in what God is without becoming God's essence.

The movement toward deification follows a threefold path in Maximus's thought: praxis (the ascetical purification of the passions through virtue), theoria physikê (the contemplation of the logoi — the divine meanings — embedded in created things), and theologia (the direct, imageless contemplation of God himself). This schema, adapted from Evagrius Ponticus and purged of his errors, maps the soul's return to God through the three stages that Origen, Dionysius, and the whole Eastern tradition recognizes as purgation, illumination, and union.

The Logoi in the Logos and the Ambigua

One of Maximus's most original and influential contributions is his doctrine of the logoi of created things. Every creature possesses a logos — a divine intention, a participated rational principle — through which it subsists in the eternal Logos. Paul's declaration that in Christ "all things were created, in heaven and on earth... and in him all things hold together" (Col 1:16–17) is the scriptural foundation. The multiplicity of logoi subsist in the one Logos without confusion, just as the rays of the sun converge in one source without mixing with each other.

This doctrine has profound consequences. First, it means that all genuine knowledge of created things is, at a deeper level, knowledge of the Logos; the sciences and arts are not theologically neutral but participate in the divine wisdom expressed in creation. Second, it means that the contemplation of nature (theoria physikê) — perceiving the logos of each thing — is itself a spiritual exercise. Third, it means that the entire created order has a Christological structure: its origin, meaning, and consummation are found in the Word made flesh.

The Ambigua ("Difficulties") — Maximus's solutions to difficult passages in Gregory Nazianzus and Pseudo-Dionysius — are perhaps his most demanding and rewarding works. In them he develops his account of the relationship between nature and will, between the logos of a nature (what a thing is by creation) and the tropos (the mode of existence by which that nature is actualized). In Christ, the human nature's logos is preserved intact, but its tropos is that of the divine Person — which is precisely why Christ's humanity is the paradigm of deified humanity.

  • John 1:1–4 — The Logos as the source of all life and light.
  • Eph 1:10 — God's plan to unite all things in Christ.
  • Rom 8:19–21 — The creation awaiting its liberation and glorification.

The Centuries on Charity

The Four Centuries on Charity are among Maximus's most accessible works, written in the tradition of the monastic kephalaia (brief, numbered aphorisms for meditation). Love (agapê) is for Maximus not merely an ethical virtue but the divine energy that moves the entire cosmos toward its consummation. "God is love" (1 John 4:8) is not a pious sentiment but an ontological claim: the inner life of the Trinity is pure self-giving love, and the creation of the world is an act of love overflowing the divine fullness.

Charity for Maximus is inseparable from dispassion (apatheia) — not the Stoic absence of feeling, but the purification of the passions so that love is no longer distorted by self-interest, fear, or pride. The monk who achieves dispassion does not become cold; he becomes capable of loving all people equally, as God loves all people equally, making his sun rise on the evil and the good (Matt 5:45). This universal charity is the sign that the soul has been genuinely transformed by grace and not merely disciplined by habit.

sections.heading: Mystical Theology and the Liturgy
sections.html:
The Mystagogy: Church, Cosmos, and Person

The Mystagogy is Maximus's most sustained meditation on the meaning of the Christian liturgy. Drawing on a method he inherited from Pseudo-Dionysius's Ecclesiastical Hierarchy but transforming it through his more incarnational and Christological theology, Maximus reads the church building, the liturgical action, and the structure of the human person as a set of nested mirrors reflecting the same divine reality.

The church building is an image of God: the sanctuary corresponds to the divine transcendence (God as he is in himself), the nave to the divine condescension (God as he relates to creatures). It is also an image of the world: the sanctuary to the intelligible heavenly realm, the nave to the sensible earthly realm. It is also an image of the human person: the sanctuary to the soul, the nave to the body. These are not arbitrary allegorical games but expressions of Maximus's conviction that the same Logos who is the source of all created logoi structures reality at every level in a single intelligible pattern.

The movement of the liturgy — gathering, procession, Scripture reading, Eucharistic offering, and communion — enacts the soul's ascent from multiplicity to unity, from the sensible to the intelligible, from the image to the archetype. Each liturgical action has both a historical meaning (referring to the events of salvation history) and a mystical meaning (referring to the soul's present transformation). The Divine Liturgy is thus simultaneously a memorial of the past, a participation in the present heavenly liturgy, and an anticipation of the eschatological communion of all things in God.

  • Heb 8:5 — The earthly sanctuary as a "copy and shadow" of the heavenly.
  • Rev 4:1–11 — The heavenly liturgy that earthly worship participates in.
  • John 4:23–24 — Worship in spirit and truth as the eschatological form of true worship.

Asceticism and the Role of Contemplation

For Maximus, the ascetical life is not a flight from the world but a progressive reordering of the human person — body, soul, and spirit — toward its original and ultimate end in God. The passions are not intrinsically evil; they are natural energies that, when disordered by sin, become destructive and when purified by virtue become vehicles of love and contemplation. Eros itself — the passionate longing that drives human beings — is ultimately a participated form of the divine Eros by which God moves all things toward himself.

Contemplation (theoria) in Maximus is not a rare achievement reserved for specialists but the natural telos of all rational creatures. Every human being is made to know God and to find rest in that knowledge. The ascetic struggle prepares the ground; the sacraments supply the grace; Scripture provides the language; and the liturgy provides the enactment. But the final movement — the crossing from active contemplation to passive union — is always a gift of God, the divine condescension meeting the human ascent.

Legacy: East and West

Maximus stands at the hinge of Eastern Christian thought. He synthesized the Alexandrian tradition (Origen, Athanasius, Cyril) with the Cappadocians and Pseudo-Dionysius, and transmitted this synthesis to later Byzantine theology. John of Damascus (d. ~749), the great systematizer of Orthodox theology, drew deeply on Maximus's Christology and cosmology. In the West, the Irish scholar John Scottus Eriugena translated several of Maximus's works in the ninth century, introducing his thought to Latin theology, where it exercised an influence on medieval mysticism.

More recently, Hans Urs von Balthasar's landmark study Cosmic Liturgy (1941) brought Maximus to the attention of twentieth-century Catholic theology, and Orthodox theologians such as Georges Florovsky and John Meyendorff placed him at the center of the neo-patristic synthesis. His insistence that the Incarnation is not merely a remedy for sin but the very goal of creation — that the Word would have become flesh even had there been no fall — gives his theology a cosmic optimism grounded not in human achievement but in the divine initiative of love. As Paul writes: "For in him the whole fullness of deity dwells bodily, and you have been filled in him" (Col 2:9–10).
